lessons.title: Hello, Swift
lessons.summary: Make your first Swift program talk to you.

payload.title: Say hello
payload.body: Computers do what you tell them. In Swift, print() shows text on the screen.

payload.prompt: What does print do?
payload.explanation: print writes text to the output, so you can see it.

payload.prompt: Finish the line to print Hi.
payload.explanation: Text in Swift goes inside double quotes.

lessons.title: Boxes called variables
lessons.summary: Store numbers and words so you can use them later.

payload.title: let and var
payload.body: A variable is a name for a value. Use let when the value will not change, and var when it might.

payload.prompt: Which keyword makes a value that can change?
payload.explanation: var is variable — it can change. let is a constant.

payload.prompt: Store the number 10 in a constant called age.
payload.explanation: age won't change, so let is best.

payload.title: Number operators
payload.body: Swift knows math. Use + to add, - to subtract, * to multiply, and / to divide.

payload.prompt: What is 6 * 2 in Swift?
payload.explanation: * is multiply, so 6 * 2 is 12.

payload.prompt: Finish so sum equals 10.

payload.title: Joining strings
payload.body: Strings are words and sentences. You can join them with +.

payload.prompt: What prints from: print("Sw" + "ift")?
payload.explanation: + joins the two pieces into "Swift".

payload.prompt: Join greeting and name.
payload.explanation: + joins strings together.

lessons.title: Making choices
lessons.summary: Use if to decide what happens.

payload.title: if statements
payload.body: An if statement runs code only when a condition is true.

payload.prompt: When does the print inside the if run?
payload.explanation: The body runs only while the condition is true.

payload.prompt: Run the code only when age is at least 13.
payload.explanation: >= means greater than or equal.

payload.title: for-in loops
payload.body: A for-in loop repeats code for each item in a range or list.

payload.prompt: What does the loop above print?
payload.explanation: 1...3 means 1, 2, 3. The loop runs three times.

payload.prompt: Loop 5 times using numbers 1 through 5.
payload.explanation: ... is Swift's closed range operator.

lessons.title: Functions do jobs
lessons.summary: Wrap code so you can call it by name.

payload.title: Declaring a function
payload.body: A function groups steps under a name. Use func, add parameters in (), and code in { }.

payload.prompt: What keyword starts a function in Swift?
payload.explanation: Swift uses func.

payload.prompt: Complete the function declaration.
payload.explanation: func declares a function.

payload.title: Make a list
payload.body: An array is an ordered list. Use [] to build one, and [index] to read.

payload.prompt: What prints from colors[0] above?
payload.explanation: Swift arrays start at index 0.

payload.prompt: Get the second item from the array.
payload.explanation: Index 1 is the second item because arrays start at 0.

lessons.title: Dictionaries: key-value pairs
lessons.summary: Look things up by name, not by position.

payload.title: A box with labels
payload.body: A dictionary stores values with named keys. Use square brackets with the key to get a value back. Perfect for scores, settings, or anything you'd look up by name.

payload.prompt: How do you look up Sam's score?
payload.explanation: Use the key in square brackets. Swift dictionaries are accessed by key, not index.

payload.prompt: Add a new name and score to the dictionary.
payload.explanation: Dictionary keys need quotes when they're strings.

lessons.title: Optionals — maybe a value
lessons.summary: Handle things that might be missing.

payload.title: ? means maybe
payload.body: An optional is a box that might have a value or might be empty (nil). The ? after a type makes it optional. Use if let to safely open the box only when there's something inside.

payload.prompt: What does nil mean in Swift?
payload.explanation: nil means the optional is truly empty — no value, not even zero.

payload.prompt: Mark the variable as optional (could be nil).
payload.explanation: ? after the type makes it optional so it can hold nil.

lessons.title: Structs — your own types
lessons.summary: Group related data into a new type.

payload.title: Bundle properties together
payload.body: A struct is a blueprint for your own custom type. It holds properties that go together — like a Player with a name and a score. Swift gives you a free initializer based on the properties.

payload.prompt: Why use a struct instead of separate variables?
options: It groups related data with a name
payload.explanation: Structs let you treat related data as one thing — cleaner code that's easier to read.

payload.prompt: Declare a struct called Book.
payload.explanation: struct is the keyword for a Swift structure type.

lessons.title: Classes and objects
lessons.summary: Blueprints for things that have behavior too.

payload.title: Classes can do things
payload.body: A class is like a struct but it can be inherited and shared. You create an object (an instance) and call methods on it. This is how big apps are built.

payload.prompt: What does init do in a class?
payload.explanation: init runs when you create a new object — it sets the initial values.

payload.prompt: Call the bark method on the dog.
payload.explanation: Method names in Swift start lowercase and use camelCase.

lessons.title: Protocols — promises your type keeps
lessons.summary: Describe what a type can do, not how.

payload.title: A shape to fit into
payload.body: A protocol lists methods and properties. Any type that adopts the protocol promises to implement them. This lets different types work together as long as they keep the same promise.

payload.prompt: What does 'Robot: Greetable' mean?
payload.explanation: The colon means Robot conforms to — adopts — the Greetable protocol.

payload.prompt: Declare a protocol called Movable.
payload.explanation: protocol is the keyword for declaring a protocol.

lessons.title: Closures — tiny functions
lessons.summary: Pass code around like a value.

payload.title: Code you can hand to something else
payload.body: A closure is a block of code in { } that you can store in a variable or pass to a function. Swift's sort, filter, and map methods all take closures.

payload.prompt: What does $0 mean inside a closure?
payload.explanation: $0 is the first argument, $1 is the second, and so on — shorthand for closures.

payload.prompt: Double every number in the array.
payload.explanation: Multiply by 2 to double each value.
